title: Stop Chasing Dopamine Hits. Start Building Something That Lasts.
description: Why chasing likes, streaks, and notifications keeps you stuck — and how channeling that same energy into creation transforms everything.
author: Be Candid Team
content:
You're not lazy. You're not unmotivated. You have enormous energy — you've just been pouring it into things designed to evaporate the moment you get them.

The new follower. The match on the dating app. The fresh notification. The next episode. The next scroll. The next hit of novelty that makes your brain light up for exactly 1.5 seconds before it asks: what's next?

That's chasing. And the tragedy of chasing isn't that it feels bad — it's that it feels almost good. Just enough to keep you running.

The Anatomy of a Chase

Chasing is the compulsive pursuit of short-term reward at the expense of long-term meaning. It's characterized by:

  • Escalation. What satisfied you yesterday doesn't satisfy you today. You need more followers, more likes, more novelty, more extreme content.
  • Emptiness after acquisition. You get the thing — the match, the viral post, the new gadget — and within hours, the excitement is gone. Nothing fundamental has changed.
  • Inability to sustain effort. When everything is optimized for instant feedback, anything that requires patience — learning a skill, building a relationship, creating something meaningful — feels unbearably slow.
  • Identity outsourcing. Your sense of self becomes dependent on external validation metrics: numbers, reactions, responses from others.

Your phone is the most sophisticated chasing machine ever built. Every app on it is designed by teams of engineers and behavioral psychologists to create loops of pursuit, acquisition, and dissatisfaction that drive you back for more.

What You're Actually Chasing

Behind every chase is a legitimate need wearing a disguise:

Chasing likes is often a search for significance — the feeling that you matter, that your existence registers in the world.

Chasing novelty — endless scrolling, new content, new rabbit holes — is often a search for vitality. Your actual life feels flat, and the scroll gives you the simulation of aliveness.

Chasing pornography or sexual content is often a search for connection and power — the feeling of being desired, of having access, of mattering to someone in an intimate way.

Chasing productivity content — buying courses, saving posts, collecting tools — is often a search for competence without the risk of actual failure.

None of these needs are wrong. They're deeply human. The problem is that chasing mimics meeting them without ever actually doing so.

The Opposite of Chasing Is Building

Building is channeling energy into creation — into things that compound over time, that exist in the world independent of your attention, that require sustained effort and reward you with genuine competence and meaning.

The difference between chasing and building is simple: chasing depletes. Building accumulates.

An hour of scrolling leaves you with nothing. An hour of writing leaves you with words. An hour of learning an instrument leaves you slightly more capable than you were before. An hour of honest conversation with your partner leaves you more known. An hour of play with your kids leaves a memory in their nervous system that will last decades.

Why Building Feels Wrong at First

If your brain has been trained by chasing — and if you've had a smartphone for more than a few years, it has — building will initially feel like punishment. Here's why:

Delayed reward. Building doesn't give you a dopamine hit in the first thirty seconds. A woodworking project, a fitness routine, a book you're writing — these don't ping you with validation. Your brain, accustomed to instant feedback, interprets this absence as failure.

Discomfort of incompetence. When you're building something real, you're bad at it before you're good at it. Chasing never asks you to be bad at anything — consumption is always effortless. Creation requires you to sit in the discomfort of not-yet-good-enough.

No audience. In the early stages of building, no one is watching. No likes, no comments, no external proof that what you're doing matters. For someone whose sense of significance has been outsourced to metrics, this silence feels existential.

These are not reasons to avoid building. They are signs that you're doing something real.

How to Shift from Chasing to Building

1. Identify Your Chase Loops

Before you can redirect your energy, you need to see where it's currently going. For one week, pay attention to the moments you feel the pull of the chase: the reflexive app-opening, the compulsive checking, the restless pursuit of more. What pattern emerges? What are you chasing — significance, novelty, validation, connection?

2. Name the Real Need

Behind every chase loop is something you actually want. Name it honestly.

These admissions are not shameful. They are the most important data you have about yourself.

3. Find One Build

Choose one thing you can build that meets the real need — however imperfectly. Not five things. One.

  • If you're chasing significance: start creating content instead of just consuming it. Write, draw, record, teach. Put something into the world that didn't exist before.
  • If you're chasing connection: invest in one real relationship. Show up consistently. Be honest. Risk being known.
  • If you're chasing vitality: start a physical practice. Your body is waiting to remind you what aliveness actually feels like.
  • If you're chasing competence: pick a skill and practice it badly for thirty days. The act of getting better at something real is more satisfying than any productivity video.

4. Protect the Build

Your chase loops will fight back. The morning you decide to write instead of scroll, the pull toward your phone will be fierce. This is not weakness — it's your brain's prediction-error system protesting the absence of expected reward.

Protect your build with structure: a specific time, a specific place, phone in another room, a commitment to show up even when it feels pointless. The first two weeks are the hardest. After that, the build begins generating its own momentum.

5. Track Patterns, Not Points

Chasing culture loves metrics — streak counts, follower numbers, daily totals. Building requires a different kind of tracking: What am I learning about myself? Where do I get pulled off course? What was happening emotionally when I abandoned the build and went back to the chase?

Be Candid approaches this the right way — structured journaling that helps you trace the emotional triggers behind compulsive behavior, so you can interrupt the chase at its root instead of battling it at the surface.

What Builders Know That Chasers Don't

People who build — who create, who invest in long-term projects, who develop real skills, who show up for relationships over years — report a quality of satisfaction that chasers never access. It's not excitement. It's not a rush. It's something quieter and more durable: the feeling of solidity.

You know who you are because you've built something. You know what you can do because you've done it. Your identity doesn't fluctuate with your notification count because it's grounded in something you made, not something you consumed.

This is what chasing promises but can never deliver: the experience of being a person with substance, with depth, with something to show for your time on earth besides a watch history.

Start Today

You have more energy than you think. You've been spending it on things designed to leave you empty. Redirect even ten percent of that energy toward building something real — a skill, a relationship, a creative project, a business, a physical practice — and watch what compounds.

The chase will still be there. But once you've tasted what building feels like, the chase starts to look like what it always was: a very expensive way to stay exactly where you are.
